coredata.dc:title: Immobilization of fenugreek β-amylase onto functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) using Box-Behnken design: Its biochemical, thermodynamic and kinetic studies
coredata.dc:creator: Agrawal, Dinesh Chand
Yadav, Anjali
Kesarwani, Rashmi
Srivastava, O.N.
Kayastha, Arvind M.
coredata.dc:description:
β-Amylase was immobilized onto GQDs using 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane and glutaraldehyde. Optimization was carried out by Box-Behnken design and binding was confirmed by SEM, AFM, FTIR and fluorescence microscopy. Predicted optimum immobilization efficiency (88.64%) was very close to actual (87.98%), which confirmed the success of the immobilization process. The immobilized enzyme showed maximum activity at pH 5.0 and 57 °C, whereas K
m and V
max were found to be 6.40 mg/mL and 714.28 μmol/min/mg, respectively. The enzyme retained 75% activity after 12 uses at 30 °C. Increased values of ΔG° ΔH°, half-life and activation energy of the enzyme inactivation (ΔEd) revealed that thermo-stability increases after immobilization and the process followed first-order kinetics (r2 > 0.96). The activation energy of catalysis (ΔEa) and ΔEd for immobilized enzyme were 22.58 and 158.99 ± 1.10 kJ/mol, respectively which revealed that denaturation of the enzyme requires a higher amount of energy rather than catalysis. Thermodynamic and fluorescence spectroscopic studies revealed that the process is non-spontaneous (ΔG > 0) and endothermic (ΔH > 0) and occurred through protein unfolding rather than aggregation (ΔS > 0). Thus increase in thermo-stability of immobilized fenugreek β-amylase and non-toxic nature of GQDs could be exploited for maltose production in beverage, food and pharmaceutical industries.

head.ce:title: Immobilization of fenugreek β-amylase onto functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) using Box-Behnken design: Its biochemical, thermodynamic and kinetic studies

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
β-Amylase was immobilized onto GQDs using 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane and glutaraldehyde. Optimization was carried out by Box-Behnken design and binding was confirmed by SEM, AFM, FTIR and fluorescence microscopy. Predicted optimum immobilization efficiency (88.64%) was very close to actual (87.98%), which confirmed the success of the immobilization process. The immobilized enzyme showed maximum activity at pH 5.0 and 57 °C, whereas K
m and V
max were found to be 6.40 mg/mL and 714.28 μmol/min/mg, respectively. The enzyme retained 75% activity after 12 uses at 30 °C. Increased values of ΔG° ΔH°, half-life and activation energy of the enzyme inactivation (ΔEd) revealed that thermo-stability increases after immobilization and the process followed first-order kinetics (r2 > 0.96). The activation energy of catalysis (ΔEa) and ΔEd for immobilized enzyme were 22.58 and 158.99 ± 1.10 kJ/mol, respectively which revealed that denaturation of the enzyme requires a higher amount of energy rather than catalysis. Thermodynamic and fluorescence spectroscopic studies revealed that the process is non-spontaneous (ΔG > 0) and endothermic (ΔH > 0) and occurred through protein unfolding rather than aggregation (ΔS > 0). Thus increase in thermo-stability of immobilized fenugreek β-amylase and non-toxic nature of GQDs could be exploited for maltose production in beverage, food and pharmaceutical industries.

ce:section.ce:para: Enzymes being versatile biocatalysts, find widespread applications in food, chemical and pharmaceutical industries [1]. The specific properties of enzymes such as chemo-, stereo- and regio-selectivity make them superior to chemical catalysts [2], which guarantee overweening applications in the field of biosensors, drug delivery and biomedicine [3–5]. β-Amylase is non-metallo exoamylase, which breaks down polyglucans at the α-1,4-glycosidic linkage and releases β-maltose and traces of β-limit dextrin [6,7]. β-Amylase mainly distributed in higher plants and also found in some microorganisms [8]. The enzyme plays crucial roles in starch degradation [7,9], early seed germination [9] and protects cells during abiotic stresses [6,10]. Due to unique properties of β-maltose such as low sweetness, high purity index and good thermal stability, it is used for the production of sugar-free sweetener (maltitol) and Diphtheria Pertussis Tetanus (DPT) vaccine in food and pharmaceutical industries [11–13]. However, the use of soluble enzyme exhibits some drawbacks such as susceptibility to attack by proteases, difficult to reuse, sensitivity to pH, temperature and denaturing agents, which limit the use of enzymes for industrial applications [14,15]. These drawbacks can be overcome by immobilizing the enzyme to a suitable matrix. After immobilization, kinetic properties of the enzyme might be altered due to the interaction between the matrix and the enzyme. Moreover, the size of particles and viscosity of medium also affect the mobility of the enzyme, substrate and their interactions [16,17].

To study the interaction of a matrix with biological agent (microbes, enzyme) a matrix should has unique properties to get maximum efficiency. The properties of an ideal matrix include low biodegradability, non-toxicity, reduction in non-specific binding to biomolecules and works as anti-microbial agent [18,19]. Various supporting matrices such as alginate [20], chitin [21], Sepharose [22], Amberlite [23], DEAE-cellulose [24], ZnO [25], Fe2O3 [26], MoS2 nano-particles [27], POT-TiO2 [28], CdSe/ZnS quantum dots [29] and graphene quantum dots [30,31] have been utilized to study the interaction with biological agents such as microbes and enzymes. Above matrices due to unique optical and physical properties, quantum dots (QDs) have been accepted as potential material in the field of computer science, electronics, aerospace and biotechnological applications such as drug delivery, biosensor and bio-imaging [32,33]. However, the toxicity associated with inorganic quantum dots opened the door to find out new alternative matrices. In this series Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) were discovered, which have unique properties such as good solubility, better surface grafting, biocompatible and non-toxic nature that make them better candidates than that of inorganic QDs for biotechnological applications [34,35]. GQDs have been successfully applied for the preparation of glucose oxidase biosensor [30].

Researchers try to find out optimum conditions under which a process provides optimal results. To achieve the goal, they have to determine the optimum level of each factor, which produces a maximum response. Thus, optimization becomes an important step in research, which decreases the cost, labor and saves the time of an industrial process. Different approaches of experiment design such as Taguchi, Response Surface Methodology (RSM), Factorial Design and Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) have been applied [36,37], among them RSM is an efficient tool for deciding the level of process parameters, which requires a lesser number of experiments to be performed. During optimization, based on analysis of variance, an optimal model is suggested, which is visualized by 2D contour and 3D response surface plots [38,39].

The objective of this study includes optimal immobilization of fenugreek β-amylase through covalent attachment using 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) and glutaraldehyde to functionalized GQDs using Box-Behnken design of RSM. Attachment of enzyme onto functionalized GQDs were characterized using SEM, AFM, fluorescence and confocal microscopy. Subsequently, FTIR analysis was carried out to characterize different chemical interactions and bonding patterns, which occurred during immobilization process. Finally, to study the nature of reaction and enzyme stability thermodynamic characterizations were carried out.

Glutaraldehyde, APTES, ANS (8-Anilino-1-naphthalenesulfonic acid) and Thioflavin-T (Th T) were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO. Chemicals such as 3,5-dinitrosalicylic acid (DNS), soluble starch and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were procured from Hi-Media. All other chemicals were commercial products of molecular biology or electrophoretic grade from Merck Eurolab. Milli-Q (Millipore, Bedford, MA, U.S.A.) water was used throughout all the experiments.

GQDs were synthesized by a somewhat modified version of the method given by Kumawat et al. [40]. Tiny pieces of Mango leaves were dipped in absolute ethanol. Mixture was stirred constantly for 8 h and the resultant solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. Suspension was collected and dried at 80 °C for 6 h to obtain the slurry. In the present investigation instead of drying through rotatory evaporator, drying was done directly by evaporation of the solution and also centrifugation was used instead of syringe filter for the separation of precipitate from the solution. Slurry was mixed with small amount of Milli-Q water and heated in 900 W domestic microwave for 5 min. Extract were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min; the resulting suspension were then allowed to dry at 80 °C for 24 h. Resulting GQDs powder was collected and used for experiments.

Dry fenugreek seeds (Trigonellia foenum graicum) were purchased from local agriculture market. For preparation of enzyme, seeds were washed and soaked overnight in extraction buffer and crushed using kitchen blender. Subsequently, the enzyme was purified using acetone fractionation (50–65%), ion-exchange chromatography followed by glycogen affinity precipitation [41].

A dispersion of GQDs (1 mg/mL) were prepared in Tris-HCl buffer, pH 7.2 and subjected to ultra-sonication for 24 h. Subsequently, the dispersion was divided into 29 aliquots according to the design of experiment as shown in Table 1 . The aliquots were equilibrated in the same buffer and treated with APTES followed by overnight incubation in dark at room temperature. To get rid of unbound APTES, all aliquots were washed (twice) in the same buffer. Thereafter, the APTES treated aliquots were activated using glutaraldehyde and incubated in dark for 4 h. Subsequently, the aliquots were rinsed with the same buffer followed by overnight incubation with the enzyme in dark at 4 °C. To obtain immobilized enzyme, centrifugation was carried out at 3000 rpm for 3 min at 4 °C. After centrifugation, the immobilized enzyme present at the bottom of the centrifuge tube was washed and stored in the same buffer at 4 °C for future studies [27].

Protein concentration was estimated by the method of Lowry et al. (1951) [42] using crystalline BSA as a standard protein. The amount of bound protein was estimated by subtracting the protein present in washing solutions from the loaded protein.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of soluble fenugreek β-amylase activity (EC: 3.2.1.2)
ce:section.ce:para: The activity of soluble fenugreek β-amylase was determined by adding 0.5 mL soluble starch (1%) with 0.5 mL diluted enzyme at 30 °C for 3 min. Subsequently, the reaction was terminated using DNS reagent (1.0 mL), followed by 5 min incubation in boiling water bath. Thereafter, the mixture was cooled down by addition of Milli-Q water (10 mL) and absorbance was recorded at 540 nm [43,44]. One unit of fenugreek β-amylase activity is defined as the amount of the enzyme, which released 1 μM of maltose from soluble starch, under given standard assay conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of immobilized fenugreek β-amylase activity (EC: 3.2.1.2)
ce:section.ce:para: Fenugreek β-amylase immobilized on GQDs were incubated with 0.5 mL of soluble starch (1%) prepared in 0.05 M sodium acetate buffer, (pH 5.0) for 3 min at 30 °C and subsequently, centrifugation was carried out at 5000 rpm for 2 min at 4 °C. Supernatant containing hydrolytic product of starch (β-maltose) was collected in a test tube and activity was determined as soluble enzyme.

The immobilization efficiency was calculated using the following formula:
Immobilization efficiency =
Specific activity of immobilized fenugreek
β ‐ amylase
Specific activity of soluble fenugreek
β ‐ amylase
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Process optimization using Box-Behnken design (BBD) of RSM
ce:section.ce:para:
The BBD of RSM is a multivariate method, which includes mathematical and statistical techniques to optimize a response that is influenced by several variables [38,45]. In this communication, to maximize immobilization efficiency, four variables namely amount of GQDs (μg), concentration of APTES (%; v/v), concentration of glutaraldehyde (%; v/v) and amount of enzyme (μg) were studied. Different models were generated and a general form of second order polynomial equation is shown below:
y =
b 0
+ ∑
b i
x i
+ ∑
b ii
x i
2
+ ∑
b ij
x i
x j
where y is predicted response variable
xi and xj are input independent variables, which influence response variable y
bo is the offset term, bi is the ith linear coefficient
bii is the ith quadratic coefficient
bij is the ijth interaction coefficient
Finally, mutual effects of variables and interpretation were carried out using the 2D contour and 3D response surface graphs [39,46].

The change in the surface morphology of the supporting material (GQDs) was studied using scanning electron microscopy by applying dried samples to a silver stub using an adhesive material followed by transferring the sample to SEM machine. Subsequently, images were recorded and analyzed at different magnification powers [47].

FTIR spectroscopy analysis was employed to identify the functional groups attached to native, APTES treated, glutaraldehyde activated and enzyme linked GQDs. The spectra were recorded in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 using Perkin-Elmer (Spectrum 100, USA) spectrometer [48].

Fenugreek β-amylase coupled GQDs and native GQDs were stained with 100 μL FITC isomer solution (1 mg/mL) prepared in dimethyl formamide (DMF). Thereafter, incubation was carried out in dark at 4 °C for 1 h. Subsequently, sample was washed with Milli-Q water (twice) and analyzed using fluorescence (Nikon DS-Fi1) and confocal microscopy (Carl Zeiss 800) [27].

Atomic force microscope (NT-MDT Russia, NTEGRA PRIMA model) a high-resolution imaging tool at nm scale was used to analyze surface morphology by applying force using silicon cantilever tip [49] in tapping mode at 0.5 Hz scan rate. One droplet of fenugreek β-amylase coupled GQDs (0.09 mg/mL) and native GQDs were placed on a glass slide and allowed to air dry. Subsequently, the dried samples were subjected to AFM image scanning within an area of 20 μm × 20 μm. Statistical analysis was also carried out by comparing average roughness and average root mean square (RMS) roughness of the both enzymes (immobilized and soluble) [50].
The amount of soluble enzyme (~100 μg) and concentration (loading capacity) of immobilized enzymes (~100 μg/725 μg GQDs) were kept constant as determined by Box-Behnken design of experiment.
To determine optimum pH, both enzymes were incubated in various pH buffer [41,44] and activity was measured using the procedure described in the above section.

To study the effect of temperature, the both enzymes (immobilized and soluble) were incubated at various temperatures ranging from 30 to 90 ± 1 °C and activity was recorded under assay conditions as described in the above section. The activation energy (ΔEa) was estimated using the slope of Arrhenius plot, which can be calculated using the following expression [51,52]
Slope = −
Δ
E a
2.303 R
where, R is the universal gas constant gas constant (8.314 Jmol−1 K−1).

To investigate reusability of the enzyme linked GQDs, activity was measured with soluble starch followed by washing (twice) with Milli-Q water. The procedure was repeated 12 times over a period of 3 days at 30 °C. The activity of first cycle was taken 100% and residual activities of other cycles were calculated. The experiments were carried out in triplicates and the data were expressed as standard deviation in the form of error bars.

Storage stability of free and immobilized fenugreek β-amylase was investigated for a period of 120 days by storing the enzyme at 4 °C and measuring the activity at room temperature. After specific time intervals (up to 120 days) a vial containing immobilized enzyme and 0.5 mL aliquot of soluble enzyme were taken out and residual activity was measured using standard assay procedure as describe in the above section. The activity of the first day was considered as 100%.

To study the effect of substrate concentrations, both enzymes were incubated with varying concentrations (1 mg/mL to 10 mg/mL) of soluble starch and activity was calculated using the procedure described in above section. Subsequently, Lineweaver-Burk plot was drawn and kinetic constants K
m and V
max were calculated using the help of the slope and y-intercept of the plot.

To examine thermal stability, both enzymes were incubated at a specific temperature (60 °C) for different time intervals. After each incubation time, small aliquots (100 μL) were taken out and then activity assay was performed using the procedure described in the above section. The same procedure was repeated for different temperatures ranging from 30 to 70 ± 1 °C in a temperature controlled water bath (MultiTemp III, GE).

The data thus, obtained from activity measurement were used to calculate kinetic parameters [53] such as multiple correlation coefficient (R2), thermal denaturation kinetic rate constant (kd), half-life (t½), the energy of enzyme inactivation (ΔEd) as well as thermodynamic parameters [54] such as changes in enthalpy (ΔH°), entropy (ΔS°) and Gibbs free energy (ΔG°). The kd was calculated using the slope obtained from the plot between ln (v/vo) and incubation time (t) using the following equation [53]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of temperature induced inactivation using spectroscopic techniques
ce:section.ce:para: Further, studies of temperature induced inactivation of fenugreek β-amylase were carried out using fluorescence spectroscopic (Varian Cary Eclipse) measurements. Intrinsic fluorescence spectroscopic measurements were carried out by exciting the sample (10 μg/mL) at 295 nm and collecting emission spectra between 300 and 400 nm with scan speed of 100 nm min−1 and response time 1 s. Both excitation and emission slit width was set to 5 nm. Baseline corrections were carried out with buffer without protein in all cases. Variation in intrinsic fluorescence spectra of the enzyme were observed w.r.t. changes in temperature [56,57]. Extrinsic fluorescence spectroscopic studies were carried out using ANS and Th T dye. ANS is a non-fluorescent dye in aqueous medium, whereas in hydrophobic environment its fluorescent intensity increases with increasing the hydrophobicity of the environment. The dye bind to hydrophobic pockets of enzymes, exposed in the intermediate state of protein whereas in folded state these ANS binding hydrophobic pockets remain buried [58–60]. Owing to these unique binding properties of ANS, it was used as a probe to differentiate intermediate state from folded or aggregated state. A stock solution of the dye was prepared in methanol and 0.1 mg/mL concentration was used to perform the experiments. The enzyme was incubated between the temperature 50–60 °C with ANS dye and excited at 380 nm and emission spectra were recorded in the range of 400 to 600 nm. The dye Th T binds to aggregates, which is formed by β-sheet present in protein. Binding of the dye with aggregates showed increased Th T fluorescence intensity as well as blue-shift in the emission spectra. In the absence of β-sheet aggregation, the dye does not bind to either folded or unfolded protein, resulting in the absence of characteristics Th T intensity peak and emission maxima. These unique properties of Th T are used to differentiate denaturation pathways followed by a protein. That is why; the dye was used in our studies to confirm the denaturation pathways followed by fenugreek β-amylase. A stock solution of Th T was prepared by dissolving 1.6 mg dye in 5.0 mL Tris-buffer having pH 7.2. 20 μL of stock solution of Th T, added to 20 μL enzyme (50 μg) and subsequently, the sample was excited at 450 nm and emission spectra were recorded in the range of 460–600 nm for different time intervals [61,62].
All the measurements were carried out thrice and results were expressed as mean ± SD, where standards deviations (SD) of mean values were <2%.
Purity of enzyme was confirmed by SDS-PAGE (Fig. S1) and size-exclusion chromatography and finally, the enzyme having specific activity of 833.33 U/mg was used for immobilization studies [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Process optimization using Box-Behnken design (BBD) of RSM
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the conditions for maximum immobilization efficiency, RSM was employed, which facilitates two types of designs namely central composite design and Box-Behnken design. It has been proved by Ferreira et al. (2007) that Box-Behnken design is more efficient than Central Composite Design [63]. That is why, in this communication, Box-Behnken design of RSM was used to optimize the four independent variables as mentioned in the previous section. Initially, preliminary experiments were performed and ranges of the independent variables, which showed favorable effect on response, were determined. Upper and lower values of these independent variables were used to perform statistical analysis using Design Expert Software version 11.0 and experiments were performed according to the Table 1. Subsequently different models were used to fit the data using analysis of variance (Table S1) and finally, based on analysis of variance; quadratic model was suggested (Table S1). Further, regression analysis was carried out to generate coefficient of the quadratic polynomial equation as shown below:

The suitability of the final model was checked by different statistical factors such as F-value, p-value, lack of fit F-value, multiple correlation coefficients (R2), adjusted R2 (Adj R2), predicted R2 (Pred R2) and adequate precision (Adeq precision) [39,46,64] and results are shown in Table 2 .

F-value is the ratio of mean square and mean squared error and it was found to be 37.15 and there are 0.0001 chances to occur this F value due to noise, which revealed that the model is significant. The p-value less than 0.0500 imply that coefficient of model term is significant and in this communication it was found to be 0.0001, which implies the suitability of the model for design space. During optimization of a process using statistical method there are chances to be included extra design points and these points generate error in the model. The lack of fit F-value measures this pure error so F-value for lack of fit must be insignificant to fit the model well. Here we showed that, it was 4.02 and there is only 9.62% chances to occur this value due to error. Thus, insignificant lack of fit revealed that the model is suitable for design of experiment. The value 0.973 of multiple correlation coefficients (R2) that is very close to 1, denotes better correlation between the observed and predicted results (Fig 1e). To fit the model well the difference between Pred R2 and Adj R2 should not be >0.2 units. In case of fenugreek β-amylase immobilized onto GQDs, this difference was found to be 0.09 units, which revealed that the model was well fitted as shown in Table 2. Adequate precision is used to measure the signal to noise ratio. A ratio of 4 is desirable to use the model to navigate the design space. We found that the ratio was 18.16, which indicates presence of enough signals to design the model.

The mutual effects of GQDs and APTES (Fig. 1a & b) as well as GQDs and enzyme (Fig. 1c & d) on immobilization efficiency were visualized using 3-D response surface and 2-D contour plots. The conditions of independent variables to obtain maximum immobilization efficiency are as follows: Amount of enzyme: 99.259 μg, functionalized GQDs: 723.736 μg, APTES: 7.392% (v/v), glutaraldehyde: 2.054% (v/v) and this predicted maximum immobilization efficiency (88.64%) was very close to actual value (87.98%), which confirms the reliability and efficiency of the model.

To confirm the immobilization onto GQDs, the surface morphology of the supporting material was analyzed using SEM. In this communication surface morphology of native GQDs (Fig. 2i A), glutaraldehyde activated GQDs (Fig. 2i B) and enzyme immobilized GQDs (Fig. 2i C) were analyzed. The white patches shown in circle (Fig. 2i C) revealed that the enzyme has been successfully immobilized onto GQDs. Similar results (changes in surface morphology) have been demonstrated by other researchers [27,64].

To confirm the immobilization of fenugreek β-amylase onto GQDs, FTIR spectra were recorded for functionalized (Fig. 2ii a), APTES treated (Fig. 2ii b), glutaraldehyde activated (Fig. 2ii c) and enzyme linked GQDs (Fig. 2ii d). Peak obtained at ~1630 cm−1 (Fig. 2ii a) revealed presence of (sp2 hybridized CC carbon) unsaturated organic support for enzyme immobilization [65]. The FTIR spectral peak obtained at ~3431 cm−1 [65] and ~1056 cm−1 (Beauchamp Spectroscopy Tables) corresponds to the presence of hydroxyl (stretching) and CO group respectively, which confirmed the presence of oxygen containing functional groups on GQDs (Fig. 2ii a). Further, the functionalized GQDs were treated with APTES, which was confirmed by peaks located at ~1046 cm−1 for Si-O-C (Fig. 2ii b) [66]. Peaks located at ~1631 cm−1 shows presence of free –NH2 group of APTES [65]. In next step, the free –NH2 group of APTES react with –CHO of glutaraldehyde, which was confirmed by the peak located at ~1684 cm−1 for –C=N stretching [65]. Peak located at ~1718 cm−1 showed free –CHO [67] of glutaraldehyde, which reacts with lysine residue of the enzyme via Schiff base formation (Fig. 2ii c). Peaks obtained at ~1506 cm−1 and ~1622 cm−1 (Fig. 2ii d) represent carbonyl amide II and carbonyl amide I bonds, respectively confirming that the enzyme has been immobilized successfully and efficiently onto GQDs [68–70].

Morphological analysis using AFM technique reveals how enzyme molecules assemble on the surface of a matrix, whereas statistical parameters such as mean roughness and root mean square roughness provide quantitative information of the surface [71]. In our study, before immobilization approximately whole surface was covered with homogenous distribution of GQDs as shown in 2-D (Fig. 3A a) and 3-D images (Fig. 3A b) and projection was absent, whereas after immobilization the uniformity of the 2-D (Fig. 3A d) and 3-D images were changed and projections were observed (Fig. 3A e). Das et al. (2017) have obtained similar results for peanut β-amylase immobilized on MoS2-NSs [27]. Moreover, roughness profile of native and enzyme immobilized GQDs showed that after immobilization there was an increase in average roughness (65.98 nm) and root mean square roughness height (86.70 nm), which confirmed that the enzyme has been immobilized successfully (Fig. 3A c & f).

To further confirm the attachment of the enzyme onto the GQDs, fluorescence and confocal microscopy were employed. It is clear from the fluorescence and confocal images that the native GQDs did not show any fluorescence signal (Fig. 3B a & 3C a) and fluorescence intensity peak (Fig. 3B b and 3C b) whereas enzyme linked GQDs showed green fluorescence signals (Fig. 3B c & 3C c) as well as fluorescence intensity peaks (Fig. 3B d and 3C d). This revealed an efficient and successful immobilization of the enzyme on GQDs. Fluorescence and fluorescence intensity peaks obtained in case of immobilized enzyme revealed the association of FITC fluorescence dye with enzyme [27].
During study state kinetics concentration of free enzyme and amount of immobilized enzyme were kept constant as mentioned in previous section.

In this communication, the optimum pH of both enzymes was determined in the pH range of 3.0–10.0. The maximum activity of soluble enzyme was present at pH 5.7, whereas in case of immobilized enzyme it was shifted toward lower pH and found to be 5.0 (Fig. 4a). Immobilized enzyme showed broader range of pH stability (4.0–9.0), which indicates that the catalysis rate becomes less sensitive to pH changes. This shift of the enzyme's pH is influenced by the interaction between charged amino acids residues of the enzyme and functional groups present on the matrix. At extreme pH due to perturbation of physico-chemical forces, conformational state of an enzyme might be altered, which leads to decrease in biological activity of the enzyme whereas broader pH range stability might be due to multipoint attachment of enzyme with matrix, which prevents the denaturation of the enzyme [72].

Biological activity of an enzyme depends on its 3-D conformation, which is very sensitive to environmental conditions in which the enzyme is being stored and may lose their biological activity quite easily. Hence, storage stability study of an enzyme, under specific conditions becomes an important criterion to determine the use of an enzyme for industrial applications. Storage stability of an enzyme may be enhanced by immobilizing an enzyme on suitable matrix and thereby reduction in the rate of enzyme denaturation [73].

Enzyme (immobilized and soluble) was stored at 4 °C and % relative activity was measured for 120 days. The immobilized enzyme retained 45% residual activity after 120 days whereas soluble enzyme lost >90% activity for the same period of incubation (Fig. 4b). These results revealed that immobilization process increases enzyme stability for longer time. Attachment of fenugreek β-amylase to functionalized GQDs via glutaraldehyde restricted free movement of the enzyme thereby decreased the rate of enzyme denaturation, which improved storage stability of the enzyme. Other researchers have also been reported an improvement in storage stability upon immobilization onto nanomaterials [26,27].

Enzymes manufacturing with low impurities and high specificity is not an easy task and each step of enzyme purification and downstream processing, significantly increases the cost of enzyme production, which also limit the availability of enzymes. Therefore, reusability of an enzyme by immobilizing it on a suitable matrix is a key solution to overcome the above problems. In this communication, fenugreek β-amylase was immobilized on functionalized GQDs via glutaraldehyde cross-linker. This multipoint attachment between enzyme and matrix helps to reuse the enzyme. The enzyme immobilized on GQDs retained 75% of relative activity, after 12 uses (Fig. 4c) while peanut β-amylase has been found to retain 80% relative activity, after 10 uses [27]. The repeated use of the immobilized enzyme weakened the binding strength between the matrix and the immobilized enzyme, which leads to loss in enzyme activity. Moreover, the conformation of the active site might be changed due to repeated encountering of the substrate to the active site of immobilized enzyme [74].

Kinetic parameters K
m and V
max for free and immobilized fenugreek β-amylase were investigated using Lineweaver-Burk plot. The K
m values for free and immobilized enzyme were calculated to be 2.56 and 6.40 mg/mL, respectively whereas V
max values were estimated to be 1111.12 μmol/min/mg and 714.28 μmol/min/mg as shown in Table 3 . These changes in Michaelis-Menten constants (K
m ) and the maximum reaction velocity (V
max ) are due to diffusion barriers as well as loss of conformational flexibility of the immobilized enzyme to bind substrate in its active site. Similar results were also obtained with peanut β-amylase immobilized onto MoS2-NPs [27].

The optimum temperature curve for soluble and immobilized fenugreek β-amylase revealed that there was no change in optimum temperature after immobilization (Fig. S2). However, immobilized fenugreek β-amylase showed higher % relative activity than that of free enzyme in the temperature range of 60–80 °C. For instance, the immobilized fenugreek β-amylase retained 52% of residual activity at 70 °C whereas the free enzyme lost >90% activity at the same temperature. These results indicated that the stability of the enzyme with respect to higher temperature is improved after immobilization. Literature showing no changes in temperature optima of β-amylase are also available [27,64].

The activation energy for the immobilized enzyme (22.58 kJ/mol) was lower than that of the soluble enzyme (29.28 kJ/mol), which revealed that immobilization improved the catalytic efficiency of fenugreek β-amylase by decreasing the activation energy, required to achieve activated complex and the process mainly controlled by mass transfer rather than kinetic control (Fig. 4c) [26,75].

Kinetic and thermodynamic studies elucidate rate of a reaction, its mechanism and provide the information on enzyme activity as well as thermal stability [76]. That is why; an industrial process can be made economically cheap and operationally fast by studying kinetic and thermodynamic parameters of an enzyme. The value of multiple correlation coefficients (r2 > 0.96) obtained from thermal stability studies showed that thermal denaturation studies followed first-order kinetics for both enzymes. It is clear from kinetic and thermodynamic data, presented in Table 4 that the value of the first-order thermal denaturation rate constant (kd) was considerably lower than the soluble enzyme, which revealed that immobilized enzyme is more stable than the soluble enzyme (Fig. 4e). The ΔEd (Fig. 4f) (158.99 ± 1.1056 kJ/mol) of the immobilized enzyme was higher than the soluble enzyme (150.49 ± 0.0936 kJ/mol), which showed that the immobilized fenugreek β-amylase required more amount of energy to denature, which is a positive indication for the improvement in thermal stability of the immobilized enzyme [77]. The half-life is the time, which is required for 50% reduction of enzyme activity and data obtained for immobilized and soluble enzymes showed that the half-life of the immobilized enzyme increased compared to the soluble enzyme as shown in Table 4. For instance, the half-life of soluble enzyme at 60 °C was 9.0 ± 0.14 min whereas immobilized enzyme displayed four-fold (28 ± 0.20 min) increase at the same temperature. These results indicated that multiple attachments between the functionalized matrix and the enzyme create a lot of resistance to be denatured and thereby increase the half-life of the immobilized enzyme. Similar results have been reported by Nagar et al. (2012) for immobilized xylanase on aluminum oxide pellets [78].

Thermodynamic parameters such as enthalpy (ΔH°), free energy (ΔG°) and entropy (ΔS°) changes of the enzyme deactivation were estimated in the temperature range 30–70 °C and data are presented in Table 4. An increased ΔH° and ΔG° values of immobilized enzyme over their soluble form were observed at all the experimental temperatures. For instance, at 60 °C, the soluble enzyme had 147.72 ± 0.93 kJ/ mol of ΔH° whereas in case of the immobilized enzyme it was 156.226 ± 1.106 kJ/ mol under similar conditions. The increased values of ΔH° and ΔG° revealed that the immobilized enzyme required a higher amount of energy for denaturation. Marin et al. (2003) have demonstrated similar results for bovine lactoperoxidase [54]. The increase in thermal stability of an enzyme after immobilized might be result of additional linkage between enzyme and supporting matrix via bi-functional agent, which provides conformational rigidity to the enzyme at higher temperature for longer time [64,79]. The data presented in Table 4 showed that ΔS° values for the soluble enzyme are smaller than the immobilized enzyme, which revealed that the soluble form of enzyme is in more ordered state and thermal denaturation occurred through protein unfolding rather than aggregation [77,80].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of temperature induced inactivation using spectroscopic techniques
ce:section.ce:para: It was observed that the intrinsic fluorescence intensity decreased with an increased temperature (Fig. 5A a). Additionally, the emission peak exhibited a red-shift from 338 nm to about 342 nm between the studied temperatures (Fig. 5A b) [56,57]. Extrinsic fluorescence spectroscopic measurement carried out using ANS showed, an increased in ANS fluorescence intensity as well as blue-shift with increased temperature (Fig. 5B a & b) [58,59]. Moreover, absence of characteristic peak (at 580 nm) of enzyme aggregation and approximate constant Th T dye intensity with time at 30 °C (Fig. 5C a & b) and 70 °C (Fig. 5C c & d) [61,62] revealed that thermal denaturation occurred through unfolding of protein rather than aggregation.

In this study, the purified fenugreek β-amylase from fenugreek was immobilized onto functionalized GQDs using APTES and glutaraldehyde. Independent variables were optimized using response surface methodology to achieve maximum response. There was only 0.66% difference between predicted and actual immobilization efficiency, which revealed that the model is significant to the design of the space. The coupling of the enzyme to glutaraldehyde activated GQDs were confirmed by SEM, FTIR, AFM and fluorescence microscopy. Results obtained after immobilization revealed that the stability of the enzyme with respect to changes in temperature and pH is improved. The immobilized fenugreek β-amylase could be reused 12 times and stored up to 120 days with the retention of 75% and 45% residual activity, respectively. Increase in K
m (2.5 times) and decrease in V
max values (0.64 times) revealed that multipoint attachment modified the 3-D conformation of the enzyme, which hinders the substrate to reach the active site amino acid residues of the enzyme. Thermal denaturation followed first-order kinetics (r2 > 0.96) having higher values of half-life (lower kd) and activation energy of enzyme inactivation (ΔEd) than the soluble enzyme, which revealed increase in the thermo-stability of the enzyme after immobilization. An increase in the positive values of changes in Gibbs free energy (ΔG°) and enthalpy (ΔH°) revealed that immobilized enzyme requires a higher amount of energy to be denatured than soluble enzyme and the process is non-spontaneous and endothermic. A positive value of change in entropy (ΔS > 0), intrinsic and extrinsic (ANS and Th T) fluorescence spectroscopic measurements revealed that thermal denaturation occurred through protein unfolding rather than aggregation. The activation energy of enzyme catalysis (22.58 kJ/mol) was found to be lower than the activation energy of enzyme inactivation (158.99 ± 1.1056 kJ/mol), which revealed denaturation of enzyme required a higher amount of energy rather than catalysis. The immobilized enzyme showed a broader range of pH and temperature operability, better thermal stability, higher storage stability, reusability and non-toxic nature of GQDs make the immobilized enzyme suitable for the production of maltose and for the fabrication of biosensor.

Dinesh Chand Agrawal: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Anjali Yadav: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Rashmi Kesarwani: Methodology. O.N.
